title: Data Types
summary: Recognize value types and convert at clear boundaries.
refresher: JavaScript values include numbers, strings, booleans, undefined, objects, and other types. `typeof` inspects a value, while `Number(...)` converts numeric text.

blocks.body: JavaScript values include numbers, strings, booleans, undefined, objects, and other types. `typeof` inspects a value, while `Number(...)` converts numeric text. Recognize value types and convert at clear boundaries.

JavaScript variables do not lock themselves to one type. The value stored at a given moment supplies the type, which is why the same variable can hold a number and later hold text. Flexible storage is convenient, but calculations become easier to trust when conversion happens at the input boundary instead of deep inside an expression.

blocks.caption: The first plus joins text; the second calculation converts before adding.
Check conversion before using the result.

blocks.body: For the corrected path, `Number("12")` produces the number twelve, the validation succeeds, and addition produces thirteen. Remember two special cases: `typeof null` reports `"object"`, and arrays are identified with `Array.isArray(value)`.

blocks.title: Watch this detail
blocks.body: A failed conversion produces `NaN`. Since its typeof result is number, detect it with `Number.isNaN(value)`.

blocks.why: JavaScript names this primitive type boolean and returns that name as text.
Numeric zero is falsy, but a nonempty string is truthy.
